summary: Gruber writes a comedic screenplay-style piece dramatizing the announcement of iTunes 5's new UI theme, which replaced the Brushed Metal look with a new, unnamed dark interface. The piece anthropomorphizes Mac OS X's UI themes as Hollywood characters — Brushed Metal as a panicking actor learning he's been replaced, his agent Mike breaking the news, and Aqua as a drunk, gloating has-been. The humor comes from mapping the real design politics of Apple's evolving interface guidelines onto entertainment industry backstabbing and ego. It's a sharp commentary on Apple's inconsistent UI philosophy during the mid-2000s transition period, when multiple competing visual styles coexisted without clear rationale.
key_points: iTunes 5 introduced a new unnamed UI theme that replaced Brushed Metal, signaling a shift in Apple's design direction
The new theme is described as a hybrid — like Unified Title and Toolbar but darker, smoother, with custom window corner radii that violate HIG conventions
Apple's UI inconsistency is satirized: Brushed Metal still clings to Finder, iChat, and Calculator while losing flagship apps
The piece highlights how Apple's own apps routinely flouted the Human Interface Guidelines they published for developers
The Aqua theme's drunken cameo underscores how far Apple had drifted from its original OS X visual identity
money_quotes: Whatever happened to 'Once you go metal, you never go back?'
This guy trashes the HIG the way Johnny Depp trashes a hotel room.
He even sports a custom radius on his window corners. No other window on the system has a shape like this.
When is the Special Event scheduled for the next version of Calculator? Oh, that's right, there is none, because no one gives a shit about Calculator.
I'm the bad-ass theme. I'm the one who flouts the Human Interface Guidelines.
Man, he doesn't even have a name. How's that for bad-ass?
He's like Unified but darker. His color is a lot like yours, but smooth and shaded like Unified. And thinner. No borders. It's a very chic look.
key_insight: Apple's mid-2000s UI inconsistency — multiple competing visual themes with no unifying rationale — was so absurd it could only be properly captured as a Hollywood backstabbing drama.
